comments.comment: Emissions of carbon monoxide from the ammonia plant are a result of combustion of natural gas as well as infrequent venting of process gas containing carbon monoxide during abnormal operating conditions. Starting up and shutting down the ammonia plant and process upsets will require venting of significant quantities of process gas containing carbon monoxide.  The concentration of carbon monoxide in the process gas varies throughout the process, so the quantity of carbon monoxide vented each year will vary depending on how many planned and unplanned outages or process upsets there were, the duration of process upset conditions (duration of venting), and the point of the process where the upset/venting occurred.  In 2014 there were two significant unplanned upset events in the ammonia plant that vented large quantities of carbon monoxide.  There was no significant venting of process gas containing carbon monoxide in 2013.

comments.comment: Carbon monoxide is released as a byproduct of combustion and may infrequently be released in vented process gas during startup and shutdown of the ammonia plants. Starting up and shutting down the ammonia plants during planned maintenance outages, or unplanned process upsets, will vent partially reformed process gas containing carbon monoxide.  The quantity and duration of venting will vary depending on the reason for the plant outage and duration of startup which can result in significant year to year variations in carbon monoxide emissions. In 2017, there was a scheduled plant outage in both ammonia plants that vented 885 tonnes of carbon monoxide during startup and shutdowns of the plants. In 2016 there were no planned or unplanned outages in the ammonia plants that required carbon monoxide venting of process gas. In 2015 there was a scheduled turnaround in one of the ammonia plants that vented nearly 400 tonnes of carbon monoxide during an extended plant startup.
